It also has 14 subtemples on the Kennin-ji precincts and about 70 associated temples throughout Japan. In 2002, the architectural setting was enhanced by a dramatic ceiling painting of two dragons by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] (1924–2012). The piece was first painted in the sport hall of a former Elementary school. This bold artwork was installed to commemorate the temple's 800th anniversary. The dragon symbolises the rain of Buddhist teachings. The [[:en:Shōkoku-ji|Shōkoku-ji]] in Kyoto also features a dragon on the ceiling of its main hall. ==Artworks== [[File:Wind God and Thunder God Screens by Tawaraya Sotatsu hi-res.png|thumb|300px|right|''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'' by Tawaraya Sōtatsu, a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]]]] Kennin-ji contains notable paintings by Tamura Sōryū{{Citation needed|date=September 2007}} and [[:en:Hashimoto Kansetsu|Hashimoto Kansetsu]]. ''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'', a pair of two-fold screens by [[:en:Tawaraya Sōtatsu|Tawaraya Sōtatsu]], are currently on display at the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]]. On the left is Raijin (雷神), a god of lightning, thunder, and storms in the Shinto religion and in Japanese mythology.[[Raijin]] On the right is Fūjin (風神) or Futen, the Japanese god of the wind and one of the eldest Shinto gods. He is portrayed as a terrifying wizard-like demon, resembling a red headed green-skinned humanoid wearing a leopard skin, carrying a large bag of winds on his shoulders.[[Fūjin]] [[File:Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku - Hodo Hall - Kennin-ji Zen Temple - Kyoto - Japan (47928781786).jpg|thumb|Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku|upright=0.6]] According to the Kennin-ji website, "Twin Dragons" by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] "commemorates the 800-year anniversary of Kenninji's founding, and a ceremony to mark its installation was given in April 2002. It measures 11.4m by 15.7m (the size of 108 tatami mats) and is drawn with the finest quality ink on thick traditional Japanese paper. It was created in the gymnasium of an elementary school in Hokkaido and took the artist just under two years to complete."{{Cite web|url=http://www.kenninji.jp/english/|title=Kenninji | the Oldest Zen Temple Kenninji}} ==See also== * [[:en:Yasaka Pagoda|Yasaka Pagoda]] * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * ''Kennin-ji: The Oldest Zen Temple in Kyoto'', undated brochure from temple * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. == External links == {{Commons Category:11]][[Category}} * [http://www.kenninji.jp Kennin-ji official web site]; {{in lang|ja}} [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html] {{in lang|en}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_kennin/ Kennin-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html Kennin-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?aid=279 Kennin-ji] * [http://www.buddhistchannel.tv/index.php?id=44,6297,0,0,1,0 The Buddhist Channel, "Kenninji key to Japan's tea culture] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Kennin-ji]] [[arz:كينين جى]] [[ca:Kennin-ji]] {{Commons category|Kennin-ji}} [[de:Kennin-ji]] [[es:Kennin-ji]] [[fa:کننین-جی]] [[fr:Kennin-ji]] [[ja:建仁寺]] [[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]] [[nl:Kennin-ji]] [[pl:Kennin-ji]] [[zh:建仁寺]] {{wikidata link|Q1440954}} [[Category:1200s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1202 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kennin-ji temples]] [[Category:Kyoto Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

جى]][[ca:Kennin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Kennin-ji]][[de:Kennin-ji]][[en:Kennin-ji]][[es:Kennin-ji]][[fa:کننین-جی]][[fr:Kennin-ji]][[ja:建仁寺]][[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]][[nl:Kennin-ji]][[pl:Kennin-ji]][[zh:建仁寺]] جى]][[ca:Kennin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Kennin-ji]][[de:Kennin-ji]][[en:Kennin-ji]][[es:Kennin-ji]][[fa:کننین-جی]][[fr:Kennin-ji]][[ja:建仁寺]][[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]][[nl:Kennin-ji]][[pl:Kennin-ji]][[zh:建仁寺]] [[arz:كينين جى]][[ca:Kennin-ji]][[sn:commons:Category:Kennin-ji]][[de:Kennin-ji]][[en:Kennin-ji]][[es:Kennin-ji]][[fa:کننین-جی]][[fr:Kennin-ji]][[ja:建仁寺]][[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]][[nl:Kennin-ji]][[pl:Kennin-ji]][[zh:建仁寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Kenninji01s1920.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Ken'nin-ji sect|WD=Q124821076}}|prefecture={{ill|Higashiyama-ku|WD=Q1203862}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.00098867|N|135.77366441|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Eisai|WD=Q366128}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Kyoto Gozan|WD=Q8193432}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1440954]] Wikidata name: Kennin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1440954]] Wikidata name: Kennin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Historic Zen Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{more footnotes needed|date=June 2014}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kennin-ji | native_name = 建仁寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = 150124 Kenninji Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kennin-ji is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|3.55|N|135|46|25.19|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} | rite = Kennin-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.kenninji.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Minamoto no Yoriie]] and {{ill|Eisai|en|Eisai|WD=Q366128|lt=Myōan Eisai}} | established = 1202 | year_completed = 19th century (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Kennin-ji'''|建仁寺|lead=yes}} is a historic {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} temple in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, and head temple of its associated branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} Buddhism. It is considered to be one of the so-called {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''}} or "five most important Zen temples of Kyoto". ==History== [[File:Kenninji01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門, or ''sammon'', main door), ''bōketsurō'' (望闕楼)]] Kennin-ji was founded in 1202 CE and claims to be the oldest Zen temple in Kyoto. The temple was a replica of public Chinese monasteries found [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]], which Japanese monks often frequented during the thirteenth century.{{Cite book |last1=Heine|last2=Wright |first1=Steven|first2=Dale S. |title=Zen Classics: Formative Texts in the History of Zen Buddhism |date=October 6, 2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0195175264|location=Oxford, United Kingdom |pages=141–142| url= https://books.google.com/books?id=HAoZ-BELposC&dq=Kennin-ji+was+modeled+after+the+Chinese+Baizhang+Monastery+of+the+%5B%5B+%22Tang%22+Dynasty%5D%5D&pg=PA142}} The monk {{ill|Eisai|en|Eisai|WD=Q366128|lt=Eisai}}, credited with introducing Zen to Japan, served as Kennin-ji's founding abbot and is buried on the temple grounds. For its first years the temple combined {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}}, {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}}, and [[Shingon]] practices, but it became a purely Zen institution under the eleventh abbot, {{nihongo|{{ill|Lanxi Daolong|en|Lanxi Daolong|WD=Q357988|lt=Lanxi Daolong}}|蘭渓道隆|Rankei Dōryū}} (1213–1278). However, the various elements that Eisai is said to have adopted from Japanese Tendai were actually elements associated with {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|WD=Q940709|lt=Chan Buddhism}} from the public monasteries of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}}. Ground plans from the Song dynasty still exist for three prominent monasteries in [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]] that Eisai visited: Tiantong Mountain, Tiantai Mountain, and Bei Mountain. These plans reveal monastery layouts that were quite diverse, accommodating a wide range of Buddhist practices. It's highly probable that Eisai drew inspiration for the Shingon Hall he constructed at Kenninji from Chinese monasteries. The Calming and Insight Hall at Kenninji may have been influenced by one Eisai encountered at Tiantai Mountain. The Zen master {{ill|Dōgen|en|Dōgen|WD=Q318064|lt=Dōgen}}, later founder of the Japanese [[Sōtō]] sect, trained at Kennin-ji. It is one of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect's headquarter temples. ===Kennin-ji school=== Kennin-ji is the main temple of the Kennin-ji branch, one of the 14 divisions of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect. The branch is regarded to have 72 temples throughout Japan, and approximately 25,000 adherents.{{cite encyclopedia |title=Kennin-ji Ha |encyclopedia=The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC |year=2002 |last=Baroni |first=Helen J. |publisher=[[Rosen Publishing Group]] |location=New York |page=188 |isbn=0-8239-2240-5 |oclc=42680558}} ==Architecture== [[File:Kenninji Kyoto07n3200.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''Hōjō'' (方丈, the Abbot's Quarters),
an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property of Japan}}]] When first built, the temple contained seven principal buildings. After suffering from fires through the centuries, it was rebuilt in the mid-thirteenth century by Zen master {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}}. In the 13th century, it was damaged by fighting during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Onin War}} as well as several unrelated fire incidents, precipitating its reconstruction during the 16th century with donations of buildings from nearby temples [[:en:Ankoku-ji (SIA)|Ankoku-ji]] and [[Tōfuku-ji]]. Due to further damage and destruction in the succeeding centuries (including the [[Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} era), many of its structures were also rebuilt during the {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} period and the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}}, {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho}} and [[:en:Shōwa (1926–1989)|Showa]] eras, such as the ''Kuri'' (rebuilt in 1814), the ''Reito-in'' (rebuilt in 1853) and the ''Kaisan-dō'' (rebuilt in 1884). Today Kennin-ji's buildings include the Abbot's Quarters (''Hōjō''), given by Ankoku-ji in 1599; the Dharma Hall (''Hatto''), built in 1765; a tea house built in 1587 to designs by tea master {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|en|Sen no Rikyū|WD=Q305477|lt=Sen no Rikyū}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; and the Imperial Messenger Gate (''Chokushimon''), said to date from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, and still showing marks from arrows. It also has 14 subtemples on the Kennin-ji precincts and about 70 associated temples throughout Japan. In 2002, the architectural setting was enhanced by a dramatic ceiling painting of two dragons by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] (1924–2012). The piece was first painted in the sport hall of a former Elementary school. This bold artwork was installed to commemorate the temple's 800th anniversary. The dragon symbolises the rain of Buddhist teachings. The {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} in Kyoto also features a dragon on the ceiling of its main hall. ==Artworks== [[File:Wind God and Thunder God Screens by Tawaraya Sotatsu hi-res.png|thumb|300px|right|''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'' by Tawaraya Sōtatsu, a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}]] Kennin-ji contains notable paintings by Tamura Sōryū{{Citation needed|date=September 2007}} and {{ill|Hashimoto Kansetsu|en|Hashimoto Kansetsu|WD=Q3128088|lt=Hashimoto Kansetsu}}. ''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'', a pair of two-fold screens by {{ill|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|en|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|WD=Q1281252|lt=Tawaraya Sōtatsu}}, are currently on display at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}. On the left is Raijin (雷神), a god of lightning, thunder, and storms in the Shinto religion and in Japanese mythology.[[Raijin]] On the right is Fūjin (風神) or Futen, the Japanese god of the wind and one of the eldest Shinto gods. He is portrayed as a terrifying wizard-like demon, resembling a red headed green-skinned humanoid wearing a leopard skin, carrying a large bag of winds on his shoulders.[[Fūjin]] [[File:Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku - Hodo Hall - Kennin-ji Zen Temple - Kyoto - Japan (47928781786).jpg|thumb|Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku|upright=0.6]] According to the Kennin-ji website, "Twin Dragons" by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] "commemorates the 800-year anniversary of Kenninji's founding, and a ceremony to mark its installation was given in April 2002. It measures 11.4m by 15.7m (the size of 108 tatami mats) and is drawn with the finest quality ink on thick traditional Japanese paper. It was created in the gymnasium of an elementary school in Hokkaido and took the artist just under two years to complete."{{Cite web|url=http://www.kenninji.jp/english/|title=Kenninji | the Oldest Zen Temple Kenninji}} ==See also== * {{ill|Yasaka Pagoda|en|Yasaka Pagoda|WD=Q11555353|lt=Yasaka Pagoda}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{ill|Heinrich Dumoulin|en|Heinrich Dumoulin|WD=Q103173|lt=Dumoulin}}, Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: {{ill|World Wisdom|en|World Wisdom|WD=Q8036507|lt=World Wisdom}}. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * ''Kennin-ji: The Oldest Zen Temple in Kyoto'', undated brochure from temple * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. == External links == {{Commons Category:11]][[Category}} * [http://www.kenninji.jp Kennin-ji official web site]; {{in lang|ja}} [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html] {{in lang|en}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_kennin/ Kennin-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html Kennin-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?aid=279 Kennin-ji] * [http://www.buddhistchannel.tv/index.php?id=44,6297,0,0,1,0 The Buddhist Channel, "Kenninji key to Japan's tea culture] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Kennin-ji]] [[arz:كينين جى]] [[ca:Kennin-ji]] {{Commons category|Kennin-ji}} [[de:Kennin-ji]] [[es:Kennin-ji]] [[fa:کننین-جی]] [[fr:Kennin-ji]] [[ja:建仁寺]] [[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]] [[nl:Kennin-ji]] [[pl:Kennin-ji]] [[zh:建仁寺]] {{wikidata link|Q1440954}} [[Category:1200s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1202 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kennin-ji temples]] [[Category:Kyoto Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

For its first years the temple combined {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}}, {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}}, and [[Shingon]] practices, but it became a purely Zen institution under the eleventh abbot, {{nihongo|{{ill|Lanxi Daolong|en|Lanxi Daolong|WD=Q357988|lt=Lanxi Daolong}}|蘭渓道隆|Rankei Dōryū}} (1213–1278). However, the various elements that Eisai is said to have adopted from Japanese Tendai were actually elements associated with {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|WD=Q940709|lt=Chan Buddhism}} from the public monasteries of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}}. Ground plans from the Song dynasty still exist for three prominent monasteries in [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]] that Eisai visited: Tiantong Mountain, Tiantai Mountain, and Bei Mountain. These plans reveal monastery layouts that were quite diverse, accommodating a wide range of Buddhist practices. It's highly probable that Eisai drew inspiration for the Shingon Hall he constructed at Kenninji from Chinese monasteries. The Calming and Insight Hall at Kenninji may have been influenced by one Eisai encountered at Tiantai Mountain. The Zen master {{ill|Dōgen|en|Dōgen|WD=Q318064|lt=Dōgen}}, later founder of the Japanese [[Sōtō]] sect, trained at Kennin-ji. It is one of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect's headquarter temples. ===Kennin-ji school=== Kennin-ji is the main temple of the Kennin-ji branch, one of the 14 divisions of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect. The branch is regarded to have 72 temples throughout Japan, and approximately 25,000 adherents.{{cite encyclopedia |title=Kennin-ji Ha |encyclopedia=The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC |year=2002 |last=Baroni |first=Helen J. |publisher=[[Rosen Publishing Group]] |location=New York |page=188 |isbn=0-8239-2240-5 |oclc=42680558}} ==Architecture== [[File:Kenninji Kyoto07n3200.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''Hōjō'' (方丈, the Abbot's Quarters),
an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property of Japan}}]] When first built, the temple contained seven principal buildings. After suffering from fires through the centuries, it was rebuilt in the mid-thirteenth century by Zen master {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}}. In the 13th century, it was damaged by fighting during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Onin War}} as well as several unrelated fire incidents, precipitating its reconstruction during the 16th century with donations of buildings from nearby temples [[:en:Ankoku-ji (SIA)|Ankoku-ji]] and [[Tōfuku-ji]]. Due to further damage and destruction in the succeeding centuries (including the [[Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} era), many of its structures were also rebuilt during the {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} period and the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}}, {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho}} and [[:en:Shōwa (1926–1989)|Showa]] eras, such as the ''Kuri'' (rebuilt in 1814), the ''Reito-in'' (rebuilt in 1853) and the ''Kaisan-dō'' (rebuilt in 1884). Today Kennin-ji's buildings include the Abbot's Quarters (''Hōjō''), given by Ankoku-ji in 1599; the Dharma Hall (''Hatto''), built in 1765; a tea house built in 1587 to designs by tea master {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|en|Sen no Rikyū|WD=Q305477|lt=Sen no Rikyū}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; and the Imperial Messenger Gate (''Chokushimon''), said to date from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, and still showing marks from arrows. It also has 14 subtemples on the Kennin-ji precincts and about 70 associated temples throughout Japan. In 2002, the architectural setting was enhanced by a dramatic ceiling painting of two dragons by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] (1924–2012). The piece was first painted in the sport hall of a former Elementary school. This bold artwork was installed to commemorate the temple's 800th anniversary. The dragon symbolises the rain of Buddhist teachings. The {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} in Kyoto also features a dragon on the ceiling of its main hall. ==Artworks== [[File:Wind God and Thunder God Screens by Tawaraya Sotatsu hi-res.png|thumb|300px|right|''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'' by Tawaraya Sōtatsu, a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}]] Kennin-ji contains notable paintings by Tamura Sōryū{{Citation needed|date=September 2007}} and {{ill|Hashimoto Kansetsu|en|Hashimoto Kansetsu|WD=Q3128088|lt=Hashimoto Kansetsu}}. ''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'', a pair of two-fold screens by {{ill|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|en|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|WD=Q1281252|lt=Tawaraya Sōtatsu}}, are currently on display at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}. On the left is Raijin (雷神), a god of lightning, thunder, and storms in the Shinto religion and in Japanese mythology.[[Raijin]] On the right is Fūjin (風神) or Futen, the Japanese god of the wind and one of the eldest Shinto gods. He is portrayed as a terrifying wizard-like demon, resembling a red headed green-skinned humanoid wearing a leopard skin, carrying a large bag of winds on his shoulders.[[Fūjin]] [[File:Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku - Hodo Hall - Kennin-ji Zen Temple - Kyoto - Japan (47928781786).jpg|thumb|Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku|upright=0.6]] According to the Kennin-ji website, "Twin Dragons" by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] "commemorates the 800-year anniversary of Kenninji's founding, and a ceremony to mark its installation was given in April 2002. It measures 11.4m by 15.7m (the size of 108 tatami mats) and is drawn with the finest quality ink on thick traditional Japanese paper. It was created in the gymnasium of an elementary school in Hokkaido and took the artist just under two years to complete."{{Cite web|url=http://www.kenninji.jp/english/|title=Kenninji | the Oldest Zen Temple Kenninji}} ==See also== * {{ill|Yasaka Pagoda|en|Yasaka Pagoda|WD=Q11555353|lt=Yasaka Pagoda}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{ill|Heinrich Dumoulin|en|Heinrich Dumoulin|WD=Q103173|lt=Dumoulin}}, Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: {{ill|World Wisdom|en|World Wisdom|WD=Q8036507|lt=World Wisdom}}. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * ''Kennin-ji: The Oldest Zen Temple in Kyoto'', undated brochure from temple * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. == External links == {{Commons Category:11]][[Category}} * [http://www.kenninji.jp Kennin-ji official web site]; {{in lang|ja}} [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html] {{in lang|en}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_kennin/ Kennin-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html Kennin-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?aid=279 Kennin-ji] * [http://www.buddhistchannel.tv/index.php?id=44,6297,0,0,1,0 The Buddhist Channel, "Kenninji key to Japan's tea culture] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Kennin-ji]] [[arz:كينين جى]] [[ca:Kennin-ji]] {{Commons category|Kennin-ji}} [[de:Kennin-ji]] [[es:Kennin-ji]] [[fa:کننین-جی]] [[fr:Kennin-ji]] [[ja:建仁寺]] [[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]] [[nl:Kennin-ji]] [[pl:Kennin-ji]] [[zh:建仁寺]] {{wikidata link|Q1440954}} [[Category:1200s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1202 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kennin-ji temples]] [[Category:Kyoto Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

description|Historic Zen Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{more footnotes needed|date=June 2014}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kennin-ji | native_name = 建仁寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = 150124 Kenninji Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kennin-ji is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|3.55|N|135|46|25.19|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} | rite = Kennin-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.kenninji.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Minamoto no Yoriie]] and {{ill|Eisai|en|Eisai|WD=Q366128|lt=Myōan Eisai}} | established = 1202 | year_completed = 19th century (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Kennin-ji'''|建仁寺|lead=yes}} is a historic {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} temple in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, and head temple of its associated branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} Buddhism. It is considered to be one of the so-called {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''}} or "five most important Zen temples of Kyoto". ==History== [[File:Kenninji01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門, or ''sammon'', main door), ''bōketsurō'' (望闕楼)]] Kennin-ji was founded in 1202 CE and claims to be the oldest Zen temple in Kyoto. The temple was a replica of public Chinese monasteries found [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]], which Japanese monks often frequented during the thirteenth century.{{Cite book |last1=Heine|last2=Wright |first1=Steven|first2=Dale S. |title=Zen Classics: Formative Texts in the History of Zen Buddhism |date=October 6, 2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0195175264|location=Oxford, United Kingdom |pages=141–142| url= https://books.google.com/books?id=HAoZ-BELposC&dq=Kennin-ji+was+modeled+after+the+Chinese+Baizhang+Monastery+of+the+%5B%5B+%22Tang%22+Dynasty%5D%5D&pg=PA142}} The monk {{ill|Eisai|en|Eisai|WD=Q366128|lt=Eisai}}, credited with introducing Zen to Japan, served as Kennin-ji's founding abbot and is buried on the temple grounds. For its first years the temple combined {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}}, {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}}, and [[Shingon]] practices, but it became a purely Zen institution under the eleventh abbot, {{nihongo|{{ill|Lanxi Daolong|en|Lanxi Daolong|WD=Q357988|lt=Lanxi Daolong}}|蘭渓道隆|Rankei Dōryū}} (1213–1278). However, the various elements that Eisai is said to have adopted from Japanese Tendai were actually elements associated with {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|WD=Q940709|lt=Chan Buddhism}} from the public monasteries of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}}. Ground plans from the Song dynasty still exist for three prominent monasteries in [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]] that Eisai visited: Tiantong Mountain, Tiantai Mountain, and Bei Mountain. These plans reveal monastery layouts that were quite diverse, accommodating a wide range of Buddhist practices. It's highly probable that Eisai drew inspiration for the Shingon Hall he constructed at Kenninji from Chinese monasteries. The Calming and Insight Hall at Kenninji may have been influenced by one Eisai encountered at Tiantai Mountain. The Zen master {{ill|Dōgen|en|Dōgen|WD=Q318064|lt=Dōgen}}, later founder of the Japanese [[Sōtō]] sect, trained at Kennin-ji. It is one of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect's headquarter temples. ===Kennin-ji school=== Kennin-ji is the main temple of the Kennin-ji branch, one of the 14 divisions of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect. The branch is regarded to have 72 temples throughout Japan, and approximately 25,000 adherents.{{cite encyclopedia |title=Kennin-ji Ha |encyclopedia=The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC |year=2002 |last=Baroni |first=Helen J. |publisher=[[Rosen Publishing Group]] |location=New York |page=188 |isbn=0-8239-2240-5 |oclc=42680558}} ==Architecture== [[File:Kenninji Kyoto07n3200.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''Hōjō'' (方丈, the Abbot's Quarters),
an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property of Japan}}]] When first built, the temple contained seven principal buildings. After suffering from fires through the centuries, it was rebuilt in the mid-thirteenth century by Zen master {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}}. In the 13th century, it was damaged by fighting during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Onin War}} as well as several unrelated fire incidents, precipitating its reconstruction during the 16th century with donations of buildings from nearby temples [[:en:Ankoku-ji (SIA)|Ankoku-ji]] and [[Tōfuku-ji]]. Due to further damage and destruction in the succeeding centuries (including the [[Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} era), many of its structures were also rebuilt during the {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} period and the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}}, {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho}} and [[:en:Shōwa (1926–1989)|Showa]] eras, such as the ''Kuri'' (rebuilt in 1814), the ''Reito-in'' (rebuilt in 1853) and the ''Kaisan-dō'' (rebuilt in 1884). Today Kennin-ji's buildings include the Abbot's Quarters (''Hōjō''), given by Ankoku-ji in 1599; the Dharma Hall (''Hatto''), built in 1765; a tea house built in 1587 to designs by tea master {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|en|Sen no Rikyū|WD=Q305477|lt=Sen no Rikyū}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; and the Imperial Messenger Gate (''Chokushimon''), said to date from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, and still showing marks from arrows. It also has 14 subtemples on the Kennin-ji precincts and about 70 associated temples throughout Japan. In 2002, the architectural setting was enhanced by a dramatic ceiling painting of two dragons by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] (1924–2012). The piece was first painted in the sport hall of a former Elementary school. This bold artwork was installed to commemorate the temple's 800th anniversary. The dragon symbolises the rain of Buddhist teachings. The {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} in Kyoto also features a dragon on the ceiling of its main hall. ==Artworks== [[File:Wind God and Thunder God Screens by Tawaraya Sotatsu hi-res.png|thumb|300px|right|''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'' by Tawaraya Sōtatsu, a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}]] Kennin-ji contains notable paintings by Tamura Sōryū{{Citation needed|date=September 2007}} and {{ill|Hashimoto Kansetsu|en|Hashimoto Kansetsu|WD=Q3128088|lt=Hashimoto Kansetsu}}. ''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'', a pair of two-fold screens by {{ill|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|en|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|WD=Q1281252|lt=Tawaraya Sōtatsu}}, are currently on display at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}. On the left is Raijin (雷神), a god of lightning, thunder, and storms in the Shinto religion and in Japanese mythology.[[Raijin]] On the right is Fūjin (風神) or Futen, the Japanese god of the wind and one of the eldest Shinto gods. He is portrayed as a terrifying wizard-like demon, resembling a red headed green-skinned humanoid wearing a leopard skin, carrying a large bag of winds on his shoulders.[[Fūjin]] [[File:Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku - Hodo Hall - Kennin-ji Zen Temple - Kyoto - Japan (47928781786).jpg|thumb|Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku|upright=0.6]] According to the Kennin-ji website, "Twin Dragons" by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] "commemorates the 800-year anniversary of Kenninji's founding, and a ceremony to mark its installation was given in April 2002. It measures 11.4m by 15.7m (the size of 108 tatami mats) and is drawn with the finest quality ink on thick traditional Japanese paper. It was created in the gymnasium of an elementary school in Hokkaido and took the artist just under two years to complete."{{Cite web|url=http://www.kenninji.jp/english/|title=Kenninji | the Oldest Zen Temple Kenninji}} ==See also== * {{ill|Yasaka Pagoda|en|Yasaka Pagoda|WD=Q11555353|lt=Yasaka Pagoda}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{ill|Heinrich Dumoulin|en|Heinrich Dumoulin|WD=Q103173|lt=Dumoulin}}, Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: {{ill|World Wisdom|en|World Wisdom|WD=Q8036507|lt=World Wisdom}}. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * ''Kennin-ji: The Oldest Zen Temple in Kyoto'', undated brochure from temple * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. == External links == {{Commons Category:11]][[Category}} * [http://www.kenninji.jp Kennin-ji official web site]; {{in lang|ja}} [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html] {{in lang|en}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_kennin/ Kennin-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html Kennin-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?aid=279 Kennin-ji] * [http://www.buddhistchannel.tv/index.php?id=44,6297,0,0,1,0 The Buddhist Channel, "Kenninji key to Japan's tea culture] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Kennin-ji]] [[arz:كينين جى]] [[ca:Kennin-ji]] {{Commons category|Kennin-ji}} [[de:Kennin-ji]] [[es:Kennin-ji]] [[fa:کننین-جی]] [[fr:Kennin-ji]] [[ja:建仁寺]] [[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]] [[nl:Kennin-ji]] [[pl:Kennin-ji]] [[zh:建仁寺]] {{wikidata link|Q1440954}} [[Category:1200s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1202 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kennin-ji temples]] [[Category:Kyoto Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

sect|WD=Q124821076}}|prefecture={{ill|Higashiyama-ku|WD=Q1203862}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.00098867|N|135.77366441|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Eisai|WD=Q366128}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Kyoto Gozan|WD=Q8193432}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1440954]] Wikidata name: Kennin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1440954]] Wikidata name: Kennin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Historic Zen Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{more footnotes needed|date=June 2014}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kennin-ji | native_name = 建仁寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = 150124 Kenninji Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kennin-ji is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|3.55|N|135|46|25.19|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} | rite = Kennin-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.kenninji.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Minamoto no Yoriie]] and {{ill|Eisai|en|Eisai|WD=Q366128|lt=Myōan Eisai}} | established = 1202 | year_completed = 19th century (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Kennin-ji'''|建仁寺|lead=yes}} is a historic {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} temple in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, and head temple of its associated branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} Buddhism. It is considered to be one of the so-called {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''}} or "five most important Zen temples of Kyoto". ==History== [[File:Kenninji01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門, or ''sammon'', main door), ''bōketsurō'' (望闕楼)]] Kennin-ji was founded in 1202 CE and claims to be the oldest Zen temple in Kyoto. The temple was a replica of public Chinese monasteries found [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]], which Japanese monks often frequented during the thirteenth century.{{Cite book |last1=Heine|last2=Wright |first1=Steven|first2=Dale S. |title=Zen Classics: Formative Texts in the History of Zen Buddhism |date=October 6, 2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0195175264|location=Oxford, United Kingdom |pages=141–142| url= https://books.google.com/books?id=HAoZ-BELposC&dq=Kennin-ji+was+modeled+after+the+Chinese+Baizhang+Monastery+of+the+%5B%5B+%22Tang%22+Dynasty%5D%5D&pg=PA142}} The monk {{ill|Eisai|en|Eisai|WD=Q366128|lt=Eisai}}, credited with introducing Zen to Japan, served as Kennin-ji's founding abbot and is buried on the temple grounds. For its first years the temple combined {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}}, {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}}, and [[Shingon]] practices, but it became a purely Zen institution under the eleventh abbot, {{nihongo|{{ill|Lanxi Daolong|en|Lanxi Daolong|WD=Q357988|lt=Lanxi Daolong}}|蘭渓道隆|Rankei Dōryū}} (1213–1278). However, the various elements that Eisai is said to have adopted from Japanese Tendai were actually elements associated with {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|WD=Q940709|lt=Chan Buddhism}} from the public monasteries of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}}. Ground plans from the Song dynasty still exist for three prominent monasteries in [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]] that Eisai visited: Tiantong Mountain, Tiantai Mountain, and Bei Mountain. These plans reveal monastery layouts that were quite diverse, accommodating a wide range of Buddhist practices. It's highly probable that Eisai drew inspiration for the Shingon Hall he constructed at Kenninji from Chinese monasteries. The Calming and Insight Hall at Kenninji may have been influenced by one Eisai encountered at Tiantai Mountain. The Zen master {{ill|Dōgen|en|Dōgen|WD=Q318064|lt=Dōgen}}, later founder of the Japanese [[Sōtō]] sect, trained at Kennin-ji. It is one of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect's headquarter temples. ===Kennin-ji school=== Kennin-ji is the main temple of the Kennin-ji branch, one of the 14 divisions of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect. The branch is regarded to have 72 temples throughout Japan, and approximately 25,000 adherents.{{cite encyclopedia |title=Kennin-ji Ha |encyclopedia=The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC |year=2002 |last=Baroni |first=Helen J. |publisher=[[Rosen Publishing Group]] |location=New York |page=188 |isbn=0-8239-2240-5 |oclc=42680558}} ==Architecture== [[File:Kenninji Kyoto07n3200.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''Hōjō'' (方丈, the Abbot's Quarters),
an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property of Japan}}]] When first built, the temple contained seven principal buildings. After suffering from fires through the centuries, it was rebuilt in the mid-thirteenth century by Zen master {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}}. In the 13th century, it was damaged by fighting during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Onin War}} as well as several unrelated fire incidents, precipitating its reconstruction during the 16th century with donations of buildings from nearby temples [[:en:Ankoku-ji (SIA)|Ankoku-ji]] and [[Tōfuku-ji]]. Due to further damage and destruction in the succeeding centuries (including the [[Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} era), many of its structures were also rebuilt during the {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} period and the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}}, {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho}} and [[:en:Shōwa (1926–1989)|Showa]] eras, such as the ''Kuri'' (rebuilt in 1814), the ''Reito-in'' (rebuilt in 1853) and the ''Kaisan-dō'' (rebuilt in 1884). Today Kennin-ji's buildings include the Abbot's Quarters (''Hōjō''), given by Ankoku-ji in 1599; the Dharma Hall (''Hatto''), built in 1765; a tea house built in 1587 to designs by tea master {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|en|Sen no Rikyū|WD=Q305477|lt=Sen no Rikyū}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; and the Imperial Messenger Gate (''Chokushimon''), said to date from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, and still showing marks from arrows. It also has 14 subtemples on the Kennin-ji precincts and about 70 associated temples throughout Japan. In 2002, the architectural setting was enhanced by a dramatic ceiling painting of two dragons by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] (1924–2012). The piece was first painted in the sport hall of a former Elementary school. This bold artwork was installed to commemorate the temple's 800th anniversary. The dragon symbolises the rain of Buddhist teachings. The {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} in Kyoto also features a dragon on the ceiling of its main hall. ==Artworks== [[File:Wind God and Thunder God Screens by Tawaraya Sotatsu hi-res.png|thumb|300px|right|''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'' by Tawaraya Sōtatsu, a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}]] Kennin-ji contains notable paintings by Tamura Sōryū{{Citation needed|date=September 2007}} and {{ill|Hashimoto Kansetsu|en|Hashimoto Kansetsu|WD=Q3128088|lt=Hashimoto Kansetsu}}. ''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'', a pair of two-fold screens by {{ill|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|en|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|WD=Q1281252|lt=Tawaraya Sōtatsu}}, are currently on display at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}. On the left is Raijin (雷神), a god of lightning, thunder, and storms in the Shinto religion and in Japanese mythology.[[Raijin]] On the right is Fūjin (風神) or Futen, the Japanese god of the wind and one of the eldest Shinto gods. He is portrayed as a terrifying wizard-like demon, resembling a red headed green-skinned humanoid wearing a leopard skin, carrying a large bag of winds on his shoulders.[[Fūjin]] [[File:Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku - Hodo Hall - Kennin-ji Zen Temple - Kyoto - Japan (47928781786).jpg|thumb|Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku|upright=0.6]] According to the Kennin-ji website, "Twin Dragons" by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] "commemorates the 800-year anniversary of Kenninji's founding, and a ceremony to mark its installation was given in April 2002. It measures 11.4m by 15.7m (the size of 108 tatami mats) and is drawn with the finest quality ink on thick traditional Japanese paper. It was created in the gymnasium of an elementary school in Hokkaido and took the artist just under two years to complete."{{Cite web|url=http://www.kenninji.jp/english/|title=Kenninji | the Oldest Zen Temple Kenninji}} ==See also== * {{ill|Yasaka Pagoda|en|Yasaka Pagoda|WD=Q11555353|lt=Yasaka Pagoda}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{ill|Heinrich Dumoulin|en|Heinrich Dumoulin|WD=Q103173|lt=Dumoulin}}, Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: {{ill|World Wisdom|en|World Wisdom|WD=Q8036507|lt=World Wisdom}}. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * ''Kennin-ji: The Oldest Zen Temple in Kyoto'', undated brochure from temple * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. == External links == [[Category}} * [http://www.kenninji.jp Kennin-ji official web site]; {{in lang|ja}} [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html] {{in lang|en}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_kennin/ Kennin-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html Kennin-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?aid=279 Kennin-ji] * [http://www.buddhistchannel.tv/index.php?id=44,6297,0,0,1,0 The Buddhist Channel, "Kenninji key to Japan's tea culture] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Kennin-ji]] [[arz:كينين جى]] [[ca:Kennin-ji]] {{Commons category|Kennin-ji}} [[de:Kennin-ji]] [[es:Kennin-ji]] [[fa:کننین-جی]] [[fr:Kennin-ji]] [[ja:建仁寺]] [[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]] [[nl:Kennin-ji]] [[pl:Kennin-ji]] [[zh:建仁寺]] {{wikidata link|Q1440954}} [[Category:1200s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1202 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kennin-ji temples]] [[Category:Kyoto Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

He is portrayed as a terrifying wizard-like demon, resembling a red headed green-skinned humanoid wearing a leopard skin, carrying a large bag of winds on his shoulders.[[Fūjin]] [[File:Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku - Hodo Hall - Kennin-ji Zen Temple - Kyoto - Japan (47928781786).jpg|thumb|Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku|upright=0.6]] According to the Kennin-ji website, "Twin Dragons" by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] "commemorates the 800-year anniversary of Kenninji's founding, and a ceremony to mark its installation was given in April 2002. It measures 11.4m by 15.7m (the size of 108 tatami mats) and is drawn with the finest quality ink on thick traditional Japanese paper. It was created in the gymnasium of an elementary school in Hokkaido and took the artist just under two years to complete."{{Cite web|url=http://www.kenninji.jp/english/|title=Kenninji | the Oldest Zen Temple Kenninji}} ==See also== * {{ill|Yasaka Pagoda|en|Yasaka Pagoda|WD=Q11555353|lt=Yasaka Pagoda}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{ill|Heinrich Dumoulin|en|Heinrich Dumoulin|WD=Q103173|lt=Dumoulin}}, Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: {{ill|World Wisdom|en|World Wisdom|WD=Q8036507|lt=World Wisdom}}. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * ''Kennin-ji: The Oldest Zen Temple in Kyoto'', undated brochure from temple * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. == External links == [[Category:Category]] * [http://www.kenninji.jp Kennin-ji official web site]; {{in lang|ja}} [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html] {{in lang|en}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_kennin/ Kennin-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html Kennin-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?aid=279 Kennin-ji] * [http://www.buddhistchannel.tv/index.php?id=44,6297,0,0,1,0 The Buddhist Channel, "Kenninji key to Japan's tea culture] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Kennin-ji]] [[arz:كينين جى]] [[ca:Kennin-ji]] {{Commons category|Kennin-ji}} [[de:Kennin-ji]] [[es:Kennin-ji]] [[fa:کننین-جی]] [[fr:Kennin-ji]] [[ja:建仁寺]] [[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]] [[nl:Kennin-ji]] [[pl:Kennin-ji]] [[zh:建仁寺]] {{wikidata link|Q1440954}} [[Category:1200s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1202 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kennin-ji temples]] [[Category:Kyoto Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[arz:كينين جى]][[ca:Kennin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Kennin-ji]][[de:Kennin-ji]][[en:Kennin-ji]][[es:Kennin-ji]][[fa:کننین-جی]][[fr:Kennin-ji]][[ja:建仁寺]][[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]][[nl:Kennin-ji]][[pl:Kennin-ji]][[zh:建仁寺]] [[arz:كينين جى]][[ca:Kennin-ji]][[co:commons:Category:Kennin-ji]][[de:Kennin-ji]][[en:Kennin-ji]][[es:Kennin-ji]][[fa:کننین-جی]][[fr:Kennin-ji]][[ja:建仁寺]][[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]][[nl:Kennin-ji]][[pl:Kennin-ji]][[zh:建仁寺]] [[arz:كينين جى]][[ca:Kennin-ji]][[sn:commons:Category:Kennin-ji]][[de:Kennin-ji]][[en:Kennin-ji]][[es:Kennin-ji]][[fa:کننین-جی]][[fr:Kennin-ji]][[ja:建仁寺]][[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]][[nl:Kennin-ji]][[pl:Kennin-ji]][[zh:建仁寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Kenninji01s1920.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Ken'nin-ji sect|WD=Q124821076}}|prefecture={{ill|Higashiyama-ku|WD=Q1203862}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.00098867|N|135.77366441|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Eisai|WD=Q366128}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Kyoto Gozan|WD=Q8193432}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1440954]] Wikidata name: Kennin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1440954]] Wikidata name: Kennin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Historic Zen Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{more footnotes needed|date=June 2014}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kennin-ji | native_name = 建仁寺 | native_name_lang = ja | image = 150124 Kenninji Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = Main Hall | map_type = Japan Kyoto Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_size = | map_alt = Kennin-ji is located in central Japan. | map_caption = | location = {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|3.55|N|135|46|25.19|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} | rite = Kennin-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.kenninji.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Minamoto no Yoriie]] and {{ill|Eisai|en|Eisai|WD=Q366128|lt=Myōan Eisai}} | established = 1202 | year_completed = 19th century (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Kennin-ji'''|建仁寺|lead=yes}} is a historic {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} temple in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, and head temple of its associated branch of {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} Buddhism. It is considered to be one of the so-called {{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''}} or "five most important Zen temples of Kyoto". ==History== [[File:Kenninji01s1920.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''[[Sanmon]]'' (三門, or ''sammon'', main door), ''bōketsurō'' (望闕楼)]] Kennin-ji was founded in 1202 CE and claims to be the oldest Zen temple in Kyoto. The temple was a replica of public Chinese monasteries found [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]], which Japanese monks often frequented during the thirteenth century.{{Cite book |last1=Heine|last2=Wright |first1=Steven|first2=Dale S. |title=Zen Classics: Formative Texts in the History of Zen Buddhism |date=October 6, 2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0195175264|location=Oxford, United Kingdom |pages=141–142| url= https://books.google.com/books?id=HAoZ-BELposC&dq=Kennin-ji+was+modeled+after+the+Chinese+Baizhang+Monastery+of+the+%5B%5B+%22Tang%22+Dynasty%5D%5D&pg=PA142}} The monk {{ill|Eisai|en|Eisai|WD=Q366128|lt=Eisai}}, credited with introducing Zen to Japan, served as Kennin-ji's founding abbot and is buried on the temple grounds. For its first years the temple combined {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}}, {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}}, and [[Shingon]] practices, but it became a purely Zen institution under the eleventh abbot, {{nihongo|{{ill|Lanxi Daolong|en|Lanxi Daolong|WD=Q357988|lt=Lanxi Daolong}}|蘭渓道隆|Rankei Dōryū}} (1213–1278). However, the various elements that Eisai is said to have adopted from Japanese Tendai were actually elements associated with {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|WD=Q940709|lt=Chan Buddhism}} from the public monasteries of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}}. Ground plans from the Song dynasty still exist for three prominent monasteries in [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]] that Eisai visited: Tiantong Mountain, Tiantai Mountain, and Bei Mountain. These plans reveal monastery layouts that were quite diverse, accommodating a wide range of Buddhist practices. It's highly probable that Eisai drew inspiration for the Shingon Hall he constructed at Kenninji from Chinese monasteries. The Calming and Insight Hall at Kenninji may have been influenced by one Eisai encountered at Tiantai Mountain. The Zen master {{ill|Dōgen|en|Dōgen|WD=Q318064|lt=Dōgen}}, later founder of the Japanese [[Sōtō]] sect, trained at Kennin-ji. It is one of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect's headquarter temples. ===Kennin-ji school=== Kennin-ji is the main temple of the Kennin-ji branch, one of the 14 divisions of the {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} sect. The branch is regarded to have 72 temples throughout Japan, and approximately 25,000 adherents.{{cite encyclopedia |title=Kennin-ji Ha |encyclopedia=The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC |year=2002 |last=Baroni |first=Helen J. |publisher=[[Rosen Publishing Group]] |location=New York |page=188 |isbn=0-8239-2240-5 |oclc=42680558}} ==Architecture== [[File:Kenninji Kyoto07n3200.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''Hōjō'' (方丈, the Abbot's Quarters),
an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property of Japan}}]] When first built, the temple contained seven principal buildings. After suffering from fires through the centuries, it was rebuilt in the mid-thirteenth century by Zen master {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}}. In the 13th century, it was damaged by fighting during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Onin War}} as well as several unrelated fire incidents, precipitating its reconstruction during the 16th century with donations of buildings from nearby temples [[:en:Ankoku-ji (SIA)|Ankoku-ji]] and [[Tōfuku-ji]]. Due to further damage and destruction in the succeeding centuries (including the [[Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} era), many of its structures were also rebuilt during the {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} period and the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}}, {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho}} and [[:en:Shōwa (1926–1989)|Showa]] eras, such as the ''Kuri'' (rebuilt in 1814), the ''Reito-in'' (rebuilt in 1853) and the ''Kaisan-dō'' (rebuilt in 1884). Today Kennin-ji's buildings include the Abbot's Quarters (''Hōjō''), given by Ankoku-ji in 1599; the Dharma Hall (''Hatto''), built in 1765; a tea house built in 1587 to designs by tea master {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|en|Sen no Rikyū|WD=Q305477|lt=Sen no Rikyū}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; and the Imperial Messenger Gate (''Chokushimon''), said to date from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, and still showing marks from arrows. It also has 14 subtemples on the Kennin-ji precincts and about 70 associated temples throughout Japan. In 2002, the architectural setting was enhanced by a dramatic ceiling painting of two dragons by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] (1924–2012). The piece was first painted in the sport hall of a former Elementary school. This bold artwork was installed to commemorate the temple's 800th anniversary. The dragon symbolises the rain of Buddhist teachings. The {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} in Kyoto also features a dragon on the ceiling of its main hall. ==Artworks== [[File:Wind God and Thunder God Screens by Tawaraya Sotatsu hi-res.png|thumb|300px|right|''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'' by Tawaraya Sōtatsu, a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}]] Kennin-ji contains notable paintings by Tamura Sōryū{{Citation needed|date=September 2007}} and {{ill|Hashimoto Kansetsu|en|Hashimoto Kansetsu|WD=Q3128088|lt=Hashimoto Kansetsu}}. ''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'', a pair of two-fold screens by {{ill|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|en|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|WD=Q1281252|lt=Tawaraya Sōtatsu}}, are currently on display at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}. On the left is Raijin (雷神), a god of lightning, thunder, and storms in the Shinto religion and in Japanese mythology.[[Raijin]] On the right is Fūjin (風神) or Futen, the Japanese god of the wind and one of the eldest Shinto gods. He is portrayed as a terrifying wizard-like demon, resembling a red headed green-skinned humanoid wearing a leopard skin, carrying a large bag of winds on his shoulders.[[Fūjin]] [[File:Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku - Hodo Hall - Kennin-ji Zen Temple - Kyoto - Japan (47928781786).jpg|thumb|Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku|upright=0.6]] According to the Kennin-ji website, "Twin Dragons" by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] "commemorates the 800-year anniversary of Kenninji's founding, and a ceremony to mark its installation was given in April 2002. It measures 11.4m by 15.7m (the size of 108 tatami mats) and is drawn with the finest quality ink on thick traditional Japanese paper. It was created in the gymnasium of an elementary school in Hokkaido and took the artist just under two years to complete."{{Cite web|url=http://www.kenninji.jp/english/|title=Kenninji | the Oldest Zen Temple Kenninji}} ==See also== * {{ill|Yasaka Pagoda|en|Yasaka Pagoda|WD=Q11555353|lt=Yasaka Pagoda}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{ill|Heinrich Dumoulin|en|Heinrich Dumoulin|WD=Q103173|lt=Dumoulin}}, Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: {{ill|World Wisdom|en|World Wisdom|WD=Q8036507|lt=World Wisdom}}. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * ''Kennin-ji: The Oldest Zen Temple in Kyoto'', undated brochure from temple * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. == External links == [[Category:Category]] * [http://www.kenninji.jp Kennin-ji official web site]; {{in lang|ja}} [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html] {{in lang|en}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_kennin/ Kennin-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html Kennin-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?aid=279 Kennin-ji] * [http://www.buddhistchannel.tv/index.php?id=44,6297,0,0,1,0 The Buddhist Channel, "Kenninji key to Japan's tea culture] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Kennin-ji]] [[arz:كينين جى]] [[ca:Kennin-ji]] {{Commons category|Kennin-ji}} [[de:Kennin-ji]] [[es:Kennin-ji]] [[fa:کننین-جی]] [[fr:Kennin-ji]] [[ja:建仁寺]] [[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]] [[nl:Kennin-ji]] [[pl:Kennin-ji]] [[zh:建仁寺]] {{wikidata link|Q1440954}} [[Category:1200s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1202 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kennin-ji temples]] [[Category:Kyoto Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

The branch is regarded to have 72 temples throughout Japan, and approximately 25,000 adherents.{{cite encyclopedia |title=Kennin-ji Ha |encyclopedia=The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC |year=2002 |last=Baroni |first=Helen J. |publisher=[[Rosen Publishing Group]] |location=New York |page=188 |isbn=0-8239-2240-5 |oclc=42680558}} ==Architecture== [[File:Kenninji Kyoto07n3200.jpg|thumb|220px|left|''Hōjō'' (方丈, the Abbot's Quarters),
an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property of Japan}}]] When first built, the temple contained seven principal buildings. After suffering from fires through the centuries, it was rebuilt in the mid-thirteenth century by Zen master {{ill|Enni|en|Enni|WD=Q739826|lt=Enni}}. In the 13th century, it was damaged by fighting during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Onin War}} as well as several unrelated fire incidents, precipitating its reconstruction during the 16th century with donations of buildings from nearby temples [[:en:Ankoku-ji (SIA)|Ankoku-ji]] and [[Tōfuku-ji]]. Due to further damage and destruction in the succeeding centuries (including the [[Haibutsu kishaku]] movement during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} era), many of its structures were also rebuilt during the {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} period and the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}}, {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taisho}} and [[:en:Shōwa (1926–1989)|Showa]] eras, such as the ''Kuri'' (rebuilt in 1814), the ''Reito-in'' (rebuilt in 1853) and the ''Kaisan-dō'' (rebuilt in 1884). Today Kennin-ji's buildings include the Abbot's Quarters (''Hōjō''), given by Ankoku-ji in 1599; the Dharma Hall (''Hatto''), built in 1765; a tea house built in 1587 to designs by tea master {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|en|Sen no Rikyū|WD=Q305477|lt=Sen no Rikyū}} for {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}; and the Imperial Messenger Gate (''Chokushimon''), said to date from the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, and still showing marks from arrows. It also has 14 subtemples on the Kennin-ji precincts and about 70 associated temples throughout Japan. In 2002, the architectural setting was enhanced by a dramatic ceiling painting of two dragons by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] (1924–2012). The piece was first painted in the sport hall of a former Elementary school. This bold artwork was installed to commemorate the temple's 800th anniversary. The dragon symbolises the rain of Buddhist teachings. The {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} in Kyoto also features a dragon on the ceiling of its main hall. ==Artworks== [[File:Wind God and Thunder God Screens by Tawaraya Sotatsu hi-res.png|thumb|300px|right|''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'' by Tawaraya Sōtatsu, a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}}]] Kennin-ji contains notable paintings by Tamura Sōryū{{Citation needed|date=September 2007}} and {{ill|Hashimoto Kansetsu|en|Hashimoto Kansetsu|WD=Q3128088|lt=Hashimoto Kansetsu}}. ''[[Fūjin|Fujin]] and [[Raijin]]'', a pair of two-fold screens by {{ill|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|en|Tawaraya Sōtatsu|WD=Q1281252|lt=Tawaraya Sōtatsu}}, are currently on display at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}. On the left is Raijin (雷神), a god of lightning, thunder, and storms in the Shinto religion and in Japanese mythology.[[Raijin]] On the right is Fūjin (風神) or Futen, the Japanese god of the wind and one of the eldest Shinto gods. He is portrayed as a terrifying wizard-like demon, resembling a red headed green-skinned humanoid wearing a leopard skin, carrying a large bag of winds on his shoulders.[[Fūjin]] [[File:Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku - Hodo Hall - Kennin-ji Zen Temple - Kyoto - Japan (47928781786).jpg|thumb|Detail of Twin Dragons Ceiling Painting - by Koizumi Junsaku|upright=0.6]] According to the Kennin-ji website, "Twin Dragons" by [[:en:Koizumi Junsaku|Koizumi Junsaku]] "commemorates the 800-year anniversary of Kenninji's founding, and a ceremony to mark its installation was given in April 2002. It measures 11.4m by 15.7m (the size of 108 tatami mats) and is drawn with the finest quality ink on thick traditional Japanese paper. It was created in the gymnasium of an elementary school in Hokkaido and took the artist just under two years to complete."{{Cite web|url=http://www.kenninji.jp/english/|title=Kenninji | the Oldest Zen Temple Kenninji}} ==See also== * {{ill|Yasaka Pagoda|en|Yasaka Pagoda|WD=Q11555353|lt=Yasaka Pagoda}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} ==Notes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * {{ill|Heinrich Dumoulin|en|Heinrich Dumoulin|WD=Q103173|lt=Dumoulin}}, Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: {{ill|World Wisdom|en|World Wisdom|WD=Q8036507|lt=World Wisdom}}. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * ''Kennin-ji: The Oldest Zen Temple in Kyoto'', undated brochure from temple * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. == External links == [[Category:Category]] * [http://www.kenninji.jp Kennin-ji official web site]; {{in lang|ja}} [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html] {{in lang|en}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_kennin/ Kennin-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/12kennin.html Kennin-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?aid=279 Kennin-ji] * [http://www.buddhistchannel.tv/index.php?id=44,6297,0,0,1,0 The Buddhist Channel, "Kenninji key to Japan's tea culture] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[en:Kennin-ji]] [[arz:كينين جى]] [[ca:Kennin-ji]] {{Commons category|Kennin-ji}} [[de:Kennin-ji]] [[es:Kennin-ji]] [[fa:کننین-جی]] [[fr:Kennin-ji]] [[ja:建仁寺]] [[my:ခန်းနင်းဂျိ]] [[nl:Kennin-ji]] [[pl:Kennin-ji]] [[zh:建仁寺]] {{wikidata link|Q1440954}} [[Category:1200s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1202 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kennin-ji temples]] [[Category:Kyoto Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Rinzai temples| ]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
